sections.heading: Why App Performance Matters
sections.content: App performance directly impacts user retention, ratings, and revenue. Android targets 60 frames per second (fps), giving each frame just 16ms to complete all rendering work. Frames that take longer cause 'jank' — visible stuttering that degrades the user experience. Beyond UI smoothness, performance encompasses startup time, memory usage, battery consumption, and network efficiency. Android provides a suite of profiling tools built into Android Studio — including the CPU Profiler, Memory Profiler, Network Profiler, and Energy Profiler — to help you identify and fix bottlenecks. Performance optimization should be data-driven: always measure first, then optimize, then measure again.

rows.cause: Too much work on the main thread — layout inflation, disk I/O, network calls, heavy computation
rows.symptom: Dropped frames, stuttering scrolling, unresponsive touch

rows.cause: Heavy work in Application.onCreate() or Activity.onCreate() before first frame
rows.symptom: Long splash screen, high TTID (Time To Initial Display)
rows.tool: Android Studio App Startup profiler, Logcat timing

rows.cause: Long-lived objects holding references to Activities, Contexts, or Views
rows.symptom: Growing memory usage, OutOfMemoryError, frequent GC pauses

rows.cause: Wakelocks, frequent alarms, background network polling, GPS overuse
rows.symptom: App appears in battery usage list, device drains faster than expected

rows.cause: Redundant API calls, large uncompressed payloads, no caching
rows.symptom: Slow data loading, high data usage

rows.cause: Pixels rendered multiple times per frame due to overlapping views
rows.symptom: GPU pressure, reduced frame rate on lower-end devices
rows.tool: GPU Overdraw tool in Developer Options

sections.heading: Android Studio Profilers Overview
items: Open profilers: Run > Profile app, or click the Profiler tab in Android Studio
CPU Profiler — records method traces and system traces to identify slow or blocking code on any thread
Memory Profiler — shows real-time heap usage, GC events, and lets you capture heap dumps to find leaks
Network Profiler — shows network requests timeline, payload sizes, and response times
Energy Profiler — shows estimated energy usage and highlights wakelocks, alarms, and jobs
All profilers work on physical devices and emulators running API 26+
Use Profile with low overhead for production-like profiling — minimal instrumentation impact
Heap dumps can be captured and analyzed inline or exported as .hprof files

sections.heading: CPU Profiler & Tracing
sections.content: The CPU Profiler in Android Studio lets you record two types of traces: method traces (Java/Kotlin instrumented or sampled) and system traces (based on Perfetto/Systrace). Method traces show exactly which methods are called and how long each takes, but add overhead. System traces have very low overhead and show thread states, rendering frames, and system-level events like garbage collection. For UI jank investigation, system traces are preferred because they show the full rendering pipeline — including Choreographer frames, RenderThread, and GPU work. Use the Flame Chart view to find the hottest methods consuming the most CPU time.

sections.heading: Tracing Your Own Code
items: Add custom trace sections to annotate your code in profiler timelines
Kotlin/Java API: android.os.Trace.beginSection("MyOperation"); // ... work; android.os.Trace.endSection()
AndroidX API (safer): androidx.tracing.Trace.beginSection("MyOperation"); // ... work; androidx.tracing.Trace.endSection()
Use try/finally to guarantee endSection() is always called: try { Trace.beginSection("load") /* work */ } finally { Trace.endSection() }
Trace sections appear as labeled blocks in the System Trace timeline
Add the tracing dependency: implementation("androidx.tracing:tracing:1.2.0")
Keep section names short and descriptive — they appear directly in the profiler UI
Nest trace sections to drill into sub-operations within a larger operation

rows.rule: Never do disk I/O on the main thread
rows.reason: Disk reads can block for 10–200ms, causing dropped frames
rows.fix: Use coroutines with Dispatchers.IO or Room's built-in async support

rows.rule: Never do network calls on the main thread
rows.reason: Network latency is unpredictable — Android throws NetworkOnMainThreadException
rows.fix: Use Retrofit with coroutines or RxJava on a background thread

rows.rule: Never do heavy computation on the main thread
rows.reason: CPU-bound work blocks the UI thread, causing ANR (App Not Responding) if > 5 seconds
rows.fix: Use coroutines with Dispatchers.Default for CPU-intensive work

rows.rule: Avoid blocking calls in the main thread
rows.reason: Thread.sleep(), synchronized locks, or blocking queues stall the event loop
rows.fix: Use non-blocking coroutines and suspending functions instead

rows.reason: StrictMode detects accidental disk and network access on the main thread at runtime
rows.fix: Add StrictMode policy in Application.onCreate() for debug builds only

sections.heading: StrictMode — Catching Violations Early
items: StrictMode is a developer tool that detects and reports policy violations at runtime
Enable only in debug builds — never in production
Thread policy — detects disk reads/writes and network access on the main thread
VM policy — detects memory leaks of Activities, SQLite objects, Closeable objects not closed

Enable in Application.onCreate(): if (BuildConfig.DEBUG) { StrictMode.setThreadPolicy(StrictMode.ThreadPolicy.Builder().detectAll().penaltyLog().build()); StrictMode.setVmPolicy(StrictMode.VmPolicy.Builder().detectLeakedSqlLiteObjects().detectLeakedClosableObjects().penaltyLog().build()) }
penaltyLog() — logs violations to Logcat
penaltyDeath() — crashes the app on violation — useful for enforcing strict discipline
penaltyDialog() — shows a dialog on violation — useful during manual QA testing

sections.heading: Memory Management & Leak Detection
sections.content: Memory leaks in Android occur when an object that should be garbage collected is still held by a long-lived reference. The most common cause is passing an Activity or View reference to something that outlives it — such as a static field, a singleton, a running coroutine, or a callback. Each leaked Activity retains its entire view hierarchy, which can be several megabytes. Over time, leaks cause heap growth, increased GC pressure, and eventually OutOfMemoryError crashes. LeakCanary is the industry-standard open-source library for automatic leak detection in debug builds — it hooks into the lifecycle and alerts you with a detailed leak trace whenever a leak is detected.

sections.heading: Memory Leak Prevention
items: Never store Activity or View references in static fields or singletons
Use WeakReference<Activity> if you must hold a reference to an Activity from a long-lived object
In Fragments, always clear View references in onDestroyView(): private var _binding: FragmentHomeBinding? = null; override fun onDestroyView() { super.onDestroyView(); _binding = null }
Cancel coroutines in onDestroy() or use viewModelScope / lifecycleScope which cancel automatically
Unregister BroadcastReceivers, listeners, and callbacks in the appropriate lifecycle method
Use applicationContext instead of Activity context for singletons and long-lived objects
Add LeakCanary for automatic leak detection: debugImplementation("com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android:2.x")
LeakCanary requires zero configuration — it auto-installs itself in debug builds

rows.description: Time from app launch to the first frame being displayed — shown in Logcat as 'Displayed' log
rows.optimization: Defer all non-critical initialization out of Application.onCreate() and Activity.onCreate()

rows.description: Time until all content is fully loaded and displayed to the user
rows.optimization: Load data asynchronously — show skeleton/placeholder UI immediately, populate when data arrives

rows.description: Heavy SDK initialization in Application.onCreate() blocks the UI before the first frame
rows.optimization: Use App Startup library (androidx.startup) to initialize SDKs lazily or in parallel

rows.description: Complex view hierarchies take longer to inflate, delaying first frame
rows.optimization: Flatten layouts, use ConstraintLayout, defer off-screen views with ViewStub

rows.description: Custom splash screens built with a separate Activity add an extra launch step
rows.optimization: Use the SplashScreen API (androidx.core:core-splashscreen) — zero-overhead system-driven splash

sections.heading: Layout Performance Optimization
items: Flatten view hierarchies — deep nesting increases measure/layout passes exponentially
Prefer ConstraintLayout — expresses complex layouts in a single flat hierarchy
Avoid nested LinearLayouts with layout_weight — each weighted child triggers an extra measurement pass
Use ViewStub for views that are rarely shown — they are inflated only when made visible: <ViewStub android:id="@+id/stub" android:layout="@layout/error_view" />; stub.inflate()
Use merge tag to eliminate redundant root ViewGroups when including layouts: <merge xmlns:android="...">
Enable GPU Overdraw visualization in Developer Options — blue = 1x overdraw (OK), red = 4x overdraw (bad)
Remove unnecessary backgrounds — if a parent and child both have the same background, remove the parent's
Use Lint's layout inspector and the Layout Inspector tool in Android Studio to analyze view hierarchies

rows.description: Calculate list differences on a background thread and dispatch only the changed items — avoids full notifyDataSetChanged()
rows.implementation: Extend ListAdapter<T, VH> which uses AsyncListDiffer internally

rows.description: Tells RecyclerView that item IDs don't change — enables better animation and view recycling
rows.implementation: Override getItemId() in your adapter to return a stable unique ID per item

rows.description: Object allocation in onBindViewHolder() runs on every bind — triggers GC pressure during fast scrolling
rows.implementation: Pre-allocate formatters, listeners, and drawables outside onBindViewHolder()

rows.description: Share a RecycledViewPool between multiple RecyclerViews with the same item types
RecyclerView automatically prefetches items during idle time — ensure onBindViewHolder() is fast enough to benefit
rows.implementation: Enabled by default — avoid blocking work in onBindViewHolder() to allow prefetch to work

sections.heading: Coroutines & Threading Best Practices
items: Use Dispatchers.Main for UI updates only — never for I/O or computation
Use Dispatchers.IO for disk, network, and database operations — backed by a thread pool sized for I/O blocking
Use Dispatchers.Default for CPU-intensive work — backed by a thread pool sized to the number of CPU cores
Use withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { } inside a suspend function to switch context for a block of work
Avoid GlobalScope — use viewModelScope, lifecycleScope, or a custom CoroutineScope that is cancelled appropriately
viewModelScope is cancelled automatically when the ViewModel is cleared
lifecycleScope is cancelled automatically when the lifecycle owner (Activity/Fragment) is destroyed
Use flow { }.flowOn(Dispatchers.IO) to emit items on a background thread and collect on the main thread

rows.practice: Use WorkManager for deferrable background work
rows.description: WorkManager respects battery saver, Doze mode, and device idle constraints
Polling with AlarmManager wakes the CPU repeatedly — use push notifications instead
rows.example: Replace polling with FCM data messages that trigger a sync only when needed

rows.description: Multiple small requests keep the radio active longer than one larger request
rows.example: Combine API calls; use HTTP/2 multiplexing via OkHttp

rows.description: Unreleased wakelocks prevent the CPU from sleeping, draining battery
rows.example: Always release in finally block: wakeLock.release() in try/finally

rows.description: Doze mode restricts background activity — use APIs that work within Doze windows
rows.example: WorkManager, FCM high-priority messages for time-sensitive wakeups

sections.heading: Baseline Profiles
items: Baseline Profiles are a Jetpack feature that pre-compiles critical code paths to native code at install time
They reduce JIT compilation overhead at runtime — improving startup time and scrolling smoothness
Generated using the Macrobenchmark library: implementation("androidx.benchmark:benchmark-macro-junit4:1.x")
Define startup and interaction journeys in a BaselineProfileRule test
Output a baseline-prof.txt file placed in src/main/ — bundled into the APK/AAB
Google Play uses Baseline Profiles to pre-compile apps during install via Cloud Profile Installation
Compose apps benefit greatly from Baseline Profiles — Compose has significant JIT overhead on first run
Combine with Macrobenchmark to measure the before/after impact of your Baseline Profile

rows.use_for: Finding slow methods, jank, and thread contention
Heap analysis, GC events, memory leak investigation
Automatic memory leak detection in debug builds
Detecting disk/network access on main thread and VM policy violations
rows.access: Enable in Application.onCreate() for debug builds

rows.use_for: Inspecting live view hierarchy and finding overdraw
Visualizing overdraw in the UI — pixel-level rendering cost
rows.access: Developer Options > Debug GPU Overdraw > Show overdraw areas

rows.use_for: Measuring startup time, scrolling performance, and interaction latency
Analyzing battery usage, wakelocks, and alarms over time
rows.access: Capture bugreport via adb; upload to Battery Historian web tool

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: App performance optimization starts with measurement — use Android Studio Profilers, LeakCanary, StrictMode, and Macrobenchmark to identify real bottlenecks before optimizing. Keep the main thread free of disk I/O, network calls, and heavy computation by using coroutines with the appropriate Dispatcher. Prevent memory leaks by clearing View references in onDestroyView(), using lifecycle-scoped coroutines, and adding LeakCanary to your debug build. Optimize startup time by deferring non-critical initialization, flattening layouts, and adopting Baseline Profiles. Reduce battery drain by using WorkManager for background tasks, avoiding polling, and batching network requests. Performance is an ongoing discipline — profile regularly, especially after adding new features.
